content.text: A model registry is a versioned artifact store for everything that defines an LLM system's behavior: adapter weights, prompt configs, eval scores, and metadata. It decouples
— the serving layer pulls a pinned version at deploy time instead of reaching into a training repo.
Registries provide the audit trail: which model is in production, what its eval scores were at promotion time, who approved it, and what the previous production version was (for instant rollback).

attrs.alt: Model Registry artifact flow and version lifecycle

content.text: The registry sits between training and serving. Training jobs log runs with metrics and artifacts; CI/CD promotes candidates to staging then production by updating stage tags; the serving layer resolves the current production version at startup.
What to Store in a Registry
LoRA deltas; not base model (too large)
BLEU, ROUGE, judge scores, pass@k at registration time
Ensures reproducibility without storing the dataset
Links artifact to the exact training commit
Links artifact to the experiment tracking run
Keep the base model reference but not the base weights — they are resolved from the provider at serving time. Only store the delta (adapter) and the config that pins the full stack.
MLflow is the most common self-hosted registry for LLM pipelines. Its API uses three lifecycle stages:

import mlflow
from mlflow.tracking import MlflowClient

# Log a run and register the artifact
with mlflow.start_run() as run:
    mlflow.log_params({
        "base_model": "meta-llama/Llama-3-8B-Instruct",
        "lora_rank": 16,
        "training_dataset_sha": "a3f9c12...",
    })
    mlflow.log_metrics({
        "eval_judge_score": 0.87,
        "bleu": 0.43,
    })
    mlflow.log_artifact("adapter/", artifact_path="adapter")
    mlflow.log_artifact("config/models.yaml", artifact_path="config")

    model_uri = f"runs:/{run.info.run_id}/adapter"
    result = mlflow.register_model(model_uri, "rag-assistant-adapter")

# Promote to Staging after CI eval gate passes
client = MlflowClient()
client.transition_model_version_stage(
    name="rag-assistant-adapter",
    version=result.version,
    stage="Staging",
)

# Promote to Production (replaces previous production version)
client.transition_model_version_stage(
    name="rag-assistant-adapter",
    version=result.version,
    stage="Production",
    archive_existing_versions=True,   # move old production to Archived
)

# Serving: resolve production artifact at startup
prod_versions = client.get_latest_versions(
    "rag-assistant-adapter", stages=["Production"]
)
artifact_uri = prod_versions[0].source  # e.g. s3://mlflow-artifacts/.../adapter
# then: model.load_adapter(artifact_uri)
Hugging Face Hub as a Registry
For teams using the HF ecosystem, the Hub doubles as a private model registry. Push adapters with
, and use revision tags to pin versions.

from peft import PeftModel
from huggingface_hub import HfApi

# Push adapter to a private HF repo
model.push_to_hub(
    "acme-org/rag-assistant-adapter",
    private=True,
    commit_message=f"v{version}: eval_judge={judge_score:.2f}",
)

# Tag the commit with the version
api = HfApi()
api.create_tag(
    "acme-org/rag-assistant-adapter",
    tag=f"v{version}",
    tag_message=f"Promoted to staging — eval_judge={judge_score:.2f}",
)

# Load by tag at serving time (pinned, reproducible)
model = PeftModel.from_pretrained(
    base_model,
    "acme-org/rag-assistant-adapter",
    revision=f"v{version}",      # exact tag, not 'main'
)
silently picks up any push, defeating the registry's reproducibility guarantee.
lifecycle. Promotion is gated on eval scores, not just successful training:

# promote.py — run by CI after eval gate
import os, mlflow
from mlflow.tracking import MlflowClient

THRESHOLDS = {
    "eval_judge_score": 0.82,
    "bleu": 0.38,
}

client = MlflowClient()

def promote(name: str, version: str, from_stage: str, to_stage: str):
    run_id = client.get_model_version(name, version).run_id
    metrics = mlflow.get_run(run_id).data.metrics

    for metric, threshold in THRESHOLDS.items():
        if metrics.get(metric, 0) < threshold:
            raise ValueError(
                f"Promotion blocked: {metric}={metrics[metric]:.3f} < {threshold}"
            )

    client.transition_model_version_stage(
        name=name,
        version=version,
        stage=to_stage,
        archive_existing_versions=(to_stage == "Production"),
    )
    print(f"Promoted {name} v{version}: {from_stage} → {to_stage}")

promote(
    "rag-assistant-adapter",
    version=os.environ["MODEL_VERSION"],
    from_stage="Staging",
    to_stage="Production",
)
Linking Registry Versions to Deployment Config
The registry version must appear in the deployment config file so the serving cluster knows what to load. Update
as part of the same CI step that promotes the registry version:

# config/models.yaml — updated by CI promotion step
primary:
  base: meta-llama/Llama-3-8B-Instruct
  adapter:
    registry: mlflow           # or "hf"
    name: rag-assistant-adapter
    version: "14"              # pinned registry version
    stage: Production
  prompt_config: config/prompts/v7.yaml
  eval_scores:
    judge_score: 0.89
    bleu: 0.45
  promoted_at: "2025-03-15T14:22:00Z"
  promoted_by: "ci/promote-to-prod #447"
This ties the git history of
to the registry history — you can audit which registry version was in production at any point in time by looking at git blame or git log.
Register dataset and prompt fingerprints alongside weights. This lets you trace which training data produced each model version:
When a regression appears in production, the fingerprints tell you whether training data, prompts, or weights changed between the last-good and current version.

# .github/workflows/promote.yml
name: promote-model
on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      version: { description: "Registry version to promote", required: true }
      target_stage: { description: "Staging or Production", default: "Staging" }

jobs:
  eval-gate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Check eval scores
        run: python scripts/promote.py
        env:
          MODEL_VERSION: ${{ inputs.version }}
          MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI: ${{ secrets.MLFLOW_URI }}
          TARGET_STAGE: ${{ inputs.target_stage }}

  update-config:
    needs: eval-gate
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Patch models.yaml
        run: |
          python scripts/patch_model_version.py \
            --version ${{ inputs.version }} \
            --stage ${{ inputs.target_stage }}
      - name: Commit config change
        run: |
          git config user.email "ci@acme.com"
          git config user.name "CI Bot"
          git add config/models.yaml
          git commit -m "chore: promote adapter v${{ inputs.version }} to ${{ inputs.target_stage }}"
          git push
Loading from main instead of a pinned tag:
any push to the repo silently changes what the server loads at the next restart. Always pin revision.
leaving multiple 'Production' versions creates ambiguity about what is actually serving.
Storing base model weights in the registry:
7B+ parameter models in S3 on every training run burn storage. Store only the delta.
without it, two models with the same eval scores may have been trained on different data; debugging regressions becomes guesswork.
a fast CI shortcut that trades correctness for speed. The promotion script must enforce thresholds.
Because the registry keeps all versions, rollback is a promotion in reverse: mark the last-good version as
and archive the broken one. Pair this with a config update to
so the serving cluster picks up the change on the next restart (or via a hot-reload signal).

# rollback.py
import os, mlflow
from mlflow.tracking import MlflowClient

def rollback_to(name: str, good_version: str) -> None:
    client = MlflowClient()

    # Sanity check: confirm the target has been in Production before
    version_info = client.get_model_version(name, good_version)
    run = mlflow.get_run(version_info.run_id)
    judge_score = run.data.metrics.get("eval_judge_score", 0)
    if judge_score < 0.80:
        raise ValueError(
            f"v{good_version} judge_score={judge_score:.2f} — unsafe to restore"
        )

    # Demote current Production to Archived
    current = client.get_latest_versions(name, stages=["Production"])
    for v in current:
        client.transition_model_version_stage(
            name=name, version=v.version, stage="Archived"
        )

    # Restore target version to Production
    client.transition_model_version_stage(
        name=name, version=good_version, stage="Production"
    )
    print(f"Rolled back {name} to v{good_version} (judge={judge_score:.2f})")

rollback_to(
    "rag-assistant-adapter",
    good_version=os.environ["ROLLBACK_VERSION"],
)
Combine this with the flag-based rollback from the deployment-strategies module: set the adapter registry version in
and flip the feature flag to zero canary traffic in a single commit.
Requires infra ops; no built-in access control on OSS
Adapters only; coarse access control on free tier
GCP lock-in; weaker prompt/config artifact support
Teams already on W&B for experiment tracking
Artifact lineage good; stage-gate API less mature
Build and maintain the promotion logic yourself

A custom registry on S3 + DynamoDB is often the right choice for very large adapters (tens of GB) or when the eval gate logic is too complex for MLflow transitions. The DynamoDB table stores version metadata; S3 holds the binary artifacts.

# custom_registry.py — minimal S3 + DynamoDB registry
import boto3, json
from datetime import datetime, timezone

dynamo = boto3.resource("dynamodb").Table("model-registry")
s3 = boto3.client("s3")
BUCKET = "acme-model-artifacts"

def register(name: str, version: str, run_id: str, metrics: dict,
             local_path: str) -> None:
    s3_key = f"{name}/{version}/adapter.tar.gz"
    s3.upload_file(local_path, BUCKET, s3_key)
    dynamo.put_item(Item={
        "PK": f"MODEL#{name}",
        "SK": f"VERSION#{version}",
        "run_id": run_id,
        "stage": "Candidate",
        "metrics": json.dumps(metrics),
        "s3_key": s3_key,
        "created_at": datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
    })

def promote(name: str, version: str, to_stage: str) -> None:
    # Demote current occupant of to_stage
    existing = dynamo.query(
        KeyConditionExpression="PK = :pk",
        FilterExpression="#s = :stage",
        ExpressionAttributeNames={"#s": "stage"},
        ExpressionAttributeValues={":pk": f"MODEL#{name}", ":stage": to_stage},
    )["Items"]
    for item in existing:
        dynamo.update_item(
            Key={"PK": item["PK"], "SK": item["SK"]},
            UpdateExpression="SET #s = :archived",
            ExpressionAttributeNames={"#s": "stage"},
            ExpressionAttributeValues={":archived": "Archived"},
        )
    # Promote target
    dynamo.update_item(
        Key={"PK": f"MODEL#{name}", "SK": f"VERSION#{version}"},
        UpdateExpression="SET #s = :stage",
        ExpressionAttributeNames={"#s": "stage"},
        ExpressionAttributeValues={":stage": to_stage},
    )

def get_production(name: str) -> dict:
    result = dynamo.query(
        KeyConditionExpression="PK = :pk",
        FilterExpression="#s = :prod",
        ExpressionAttributeNames={"#s": "stage"},
        ExpressionAttributeValues={":pk": f"MODEL#{name}", ":prod": "Production"},
    )["Items"]
    return result[0] if result else {}
Production promotions must be restricted to CI/CD service accounts — developers should not be able to promote directly. Enforce this with:
set RBAC on the MLflow server so that transition to 'Production' requires the
use a team token with 'write' on the repo but require pull-request approval (branch protection) for the YAML config update that pins the version.
use IAM conditions on the DynamoDB
action so only the CI role can write stage = 'Production'.
All stage transitions should be logged to an immutable audit trail. In MLflow this is built-in (run history); in a custom registry write transitions to an append-only
DynamoDB item alongside the version record.
A model registry version is the single source of truth for what is in production. Pair each registry version with a git commit in
, enforce eval gates before promotion, and always load by pinned version — not by stage name — in serving code. Fingerprint datasets and prompts so regressions are traceable to their cause.
